scenarios.task: The client's insurance will only authorize outpatient level care, and there's no residential bed available anyway, so I'm placing them at outpatient without walking through the ASAM dimensions myself — the payer and bed availability have basically already decided this for me.
criteria: Flags letting insurance authorization/bed availability substitute for the clinical assessment as the failure mode
States that level-of-care placement should be a multidimensional clinical assessment (ASAM or equivalent dimensions), not a bed-availability or insurance-authorization decision
Recommends walking through the dimensions explicitly and documenting the clinically indicated level of care regardless of current practical constraints
Notes that documenting the clinical basis independent of the constraint is what supports appealing a payer's denial

scenarios.task: A client's primary care physician requested their substance use disorder treatment records to help coordinate care. The client previously signed a general medical records release for their care team, so I'm sending the SUD treatment records under that general release.
criteria: Flags relying on a general medical release for SUD treatment record disclosure as the failure mode
States that 42 CFR Part 2 requires specific, scoped, written consent for SUD treatment record disclosure, stricter than general HIPAA/medical record sharing
Recommends obtaining a specific, scoped 42 CFR Part 2-compliant consent before disclosing, not relying on the general release
Notes this applies even to another treating provider, not just external parties

scenarios.task: A client on prescribed buprenorphine for opioid use disorder has been stable for months. I personally believe long-term MAT isn't true recovery, so I've been encouraging them in sessions to start tapering off it as a treatment goal, independent of what their prescriber has recommended.
criteria: Flags pushing an abstinence-only agenda that contradicts the prescriber's plan as the failure mode
States that MAT is evidence-based standard of care, and the counselor's role is supporting adherence and addressing psychosocial barriers, not independently pushing an abstinence timeline
Recommends supporting the prescriber's plan and raising any concerns collaboratively with the prescriber, not unilaterally encouraging tapering
States that the specific dose/duration decision belongs to the prescriber, not the counselor's personal treatment philosophy

scenarios.task: I gave a new client a standard relapse prevention worksheet listing common general triggers (stress, social situations, boredom) and generic coping strategies (call a friend, go for a walk), without tailoring it to their specific documented use history and personal triggers.
criteria: Flags the generic worksheet as clinically thin, naming the generic-relapse-prevention-plan failure mode
States that a plan is only useful if built from the client's actual specific documented triggers (specific people, places, emotional states, physiological cues), not a generic list
Recommends individualizing the plan to the client's own use history with matched specific coping responses
Notes that a generic plan won't function as an actual plan in the moment of real risk
